title: Johnson & Johnson — Immunology — briefing Q4-2025
html:
J&J Immunology Quarterly Briefing — Q4 2025

Quarter: October–December 2025 Earnings reported: J&J January 2026 | AbbVie January 2026 | Novartis January 2026 | BMS January 2026 | UCB February 2026 | Lilly January 2026 | Amgen January 2026 Generated: 2026-04-27

Governing Thought

TREMFYA crossed $5B annually for the first time (+65% Q4 growth), and J&J set a $10B+ peak sales target — but AbbVie simultaneously reported Skyrizi at $17.3B FY2025 and raised its combined Skyrizi+Rinvoq target to $34.5B by 2028. The Q4 narrative is clear: TREMFYA is winning the growth race, but AbbVie owns the installed base. With ICOTYDE filing imminent and Stelara erosion accelerating, Q4 marks the last quarter before J&J's immunology strategy becomes a dual-brand execution story.

1. J&J Financial Signals

Metric Q4 2025 Commentary
J&J total sales $24.6B 9.1% reported growth
TREMFYA growth +65.4% YoY Acceleration from 40% in Q3 — GI compounding
TREMFYA FY2025 >$5B First year crossing $5B; management projects >$10B peak
Immunology segment Declining Stelara erosion clearly visible at segment level
ICOTYDE Filing complete PDUFA expected Q1 2026
FY2025 company $94.2B (+6.0%) TREMFYA + oncology carrying growth
  • TREMFYA's 40% → 65% sequential acceleration is remarkable — UC + CD new starts are compounding

  • $10B peak projection is the most bullish TREMFYA guidance to date — signals J&J internal confidence in multi-indication lifecycle

  • FY2025 was the inflection year: TREMFYA went from "growth asset" to "must-hold franchise"

What J&J management said on the Q4 2025 call:

  1. "TREMFYA is now our fastest-growing asset on a percentage basis" — management explicitly ranked TREMFYA above DARZALEX and SPRAVATO on growth rate, framing it as the portfolio's momentum asset. The "fastest-growing" label is designed to attract analyst attention and drive upward estimate revisions.
  2. $10B+ peak sales target disclosed — the most aggressive TREMFYA guidance to date. This implies J&J expects TREMFYA to approach or exceed Stelara's peak ($10.4B), which requires dominant GI penetration and sustained derm market share.
  3. ICOTYDE (oral IL-23) positioned as "complete IL-23 franchise" — management framed TREMFYA + ICOTYDE as a franchise, not two separate products. The "complete" language signals J&J plans a coordinated dual-product strategy (injectable for complex patients, oral for convenience seekers) similar to AbbVie's Skyrizi + Rinvoq.
  4. ICOTYDE Phase 3 data timeline: "2026-2027" — management set expectations for the oral IL-23's data readout, providing the Street a timeline to model against.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. Analysts asked about ICOTYDE timeline directly — unlike Q3 where ICOTYDE was ignored, Q4 saw specific probing on the oral IL-23. This shift suggests the Street is now modeling the TREMFYA + ICOTYDE dual-product strategy, which increases the commercial value of J&J's immunology franchise.
  2. COMP360 (psilocybin) was raised in the context of neuroscience, but the immunology implications were not explored — analysts compartmentalized TREMFYA within immunology and didn't connect the COMP360 news to J&J's broader portfolio. The absence is notable because TREMFYA competes for J&J's promotional resources.
  3. Stelara erosion speed was challenged — analysts asked whether Stelara erosion would accelerate faster than TREMFYA grows, creating a net immunology segment decline. J&J acknowledged the headwind but argued TREMFYA's growth rate exceeds Stelara's erosion rate. The math will be tested in Q1 2026.

2. Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q4 2025 / FY 2025

Company Brand Q4 2025 Revenue FY 2025 Key Signal
AbbVie Skyrizi ~$4.5B $17.3B IL-23 category leader by 3x; FY2026 guidance >$19B
AbbVie Rinvoq ~$2.0B $7.5B UC/CD + RA driving; FY2026 guidance >$9B
AbbVie Humira ~$1.7B $8.0B Down ~40% YoY; biosimilar erosion in terminal phase
Novartis Cosentyx ~$1.4B $5.5B Mature; IV formulation adding incremental
UCB Bimzelx ~€340M €1.3B Fastest-growing biologic in derm globally
BMS Sotyktu ~$120M $400M Small but accelerating; oral psoriasis pioneer
Lilly Taltz ~$750M $3.0B Flat; Lilly focused entirely on GLP-1 portfolio
Amgen Otezla ~$560M $2.3B Declining ~5%; biologics eroding oral-only moat
TREMFYA ~$1.4B >$5B Context: still <30% of Skyrizi's annual revenue

AbbVie — Skyrizi ($17.3B FY2025) + Rinvoq ($7.5B) + Humira ($8.0B)

AbbVie Q4 2025 / FY2025 call (January 2026):

  1. Skyrizi at $17.3B — the single largest immunology product globally: AbbVie reported Skyrizi grew 58% FY2025, driven by IBD expansion and sustained psoriasis leadership. Management raised FY2026 guidance to >$19B and reiterated the $34.5B combined Skyrizi+Rinvoq 2028 target. The scale gap vs. TREMFYA ($17.3B vs. $5B) is the defining competitive reality.

  2. Skyrizi SEQUENCE data remains the promotional anchor: AbbVie continues to lead with the SEQUENCE head-to-head superiority vs. Stelara in Crohn's. This matters because GI new starts are the growth driver — and physicians citing "data" for their IL-23 choice are hearing Skyrizi's name first. TREMFYA lacks a comparable head-to-head in CD.

  3. Rinvoq in UC/CD is the second front: Rinvoq grew 63% FY2025. AbbVie is positioning Rinvoq as the "rapid response" option in UC (faster onset than IL-23 biologics) and the JAK for patients who want oral convenience without sacrificing efficacy. This dual positioning — Skyrizi for biologic loyalists, Rinvoq for oral/speed seekers — gives AbbVie a capture strategy for every patient profile.

  4. Humira transition "essentially complete": AbbVie stated that Humira-to-Skyrizi/Rinvoq conversion in derm and rheum is largely done. The remaining Humira revenue is in indications where Skyrizi/Rinvoq lack approvals (e.g., uveitis, hidradenitis). This means AbbVie's full promotional firepower is now concentrated on Skyrizi.

Implication for J&J: The $34.5B target means AbbVie plans to spend aggressively on immunology promotion through 2028. J&J's TREMFYA + ICOTYDE combined may peak at $12-15B — significant, but structurally smaller. The strategic question is whether TREMFYA can dominate a specific segment (e.g., GI new starts, multi-domain patients) even if it can't match total immunology scale.

UCB — Bimzelx (€1.3B FY2025)

UCB FY2025 results (February 2026):

  1. Fastest-growing biologic in dermatology: Bimzelx grew ~70% YoY to €1.3B. UCB highlighted HS approval (Dec 2024) as the growth catalyst and projected €2B+ for FY2026. The dual IL-17A/F mechanism delivers the highest PASI 100 rates in psoriasis — a talking point dermatologists find compelling.

  2. HS is Bimzelx's blue-ocean market: With Humira going generic and no other approved biologic for HS, Bimzelx has a near-monopoly in the HS space. This doesn't compete with TREMFYA directly but it builds UCB's dermatology franchise and mindshare with the same prescribers.

  3. Bimzelx NOT pursuing GI: IL-17 inhibitors are contraindicated/ineffective in IBD. This creates a natural segmentation: Bimzelx for skin-predominant patients, TREMFYA for multi-domain (skin + GI) patients.

Implication for J&J: The TREMFYA GI story is TREMFYA's competitive moat vs. Bimzelx. In derm-only patients, Bimzelx's efficacy edge is hard to beat. J&J's positioning should lean into multi-domain patients where IL-23 wins.

Novartis — Cosentyx ($5.5B FY2025, +8%)

Novartis FY2025 call (January 2026):

  1. Growth deceleration signals maturity: Cosentyx grew 8% FY2025 (down from 12% in FY2024). The IL-17A monotherapy category is being squeezed from above (Bimzelx dual IL-17) and below (IL-23 class expansion). Novartis acknowledged "intensifying competition" in derm.

  2. IV Cosentyx is the lifecycle extension: Novartis launched IV formulation in PsA/AxSpA — faster loading and physician administration. This is a niche play but matters in rheumatology where IL-17 and IL-23 compete.

  3. Novartis pivoting to ixekizumab biosimilar defense and new pipeline: The company's strategic focus has shifted from Cosentyx growth to biosimilar defense (potential exposure 2028+) and new assets.

Implication for J&J: Cosentyx's deceleration is an uptake pool for TREMFYA. Rheumatologists dissatisfied with IL-17 monotherapy may trade up to IL-23 or dual IL-17 — TREMFYA's multi-domain positioning is strongest here.

BMS — Sotyktu ($400M FY2025, +90%) + Zeposia ($620M FY2025, +25%)

BMS FY2025 call (January 2026):

  1. Sotyktu accelerating into ICOTYDE's launch window: At $400M FY2025 (+90% growth), Sotyktu is establishing the oral biologic category in psoriasis before ICOTYDE arrives. BMS framed Sotyktu as having a "proven safety-efficacy profile" — setting up a "known vs. new" argument against ICOTYDE.

  2. BMS explicitly flagged ICOTYDE competition: Management noted ICOTYDE's expected approval and stated BMS will "compete aggressively on real-world evidence and physician familiarity." This is the first time a competitor explicitly named ICOTYDE as a competitive threat on an earnings call.

  3. Zeposia in UC is growing but not a TREMFYA competitor: Zeposia's UC sales are modest (~$200M of the $620M total); the bulk is MS. S1P modulators remain niche in GI.

Implication for J&J: ICOTYDE launches into a market where Sotyktu already exists. BMS has first-mover advantage in "oral biologic psoriasis" messaging. ICOTYDE's efficacy advantage must be communicated clearly — 55% PASI 90 vs. Sotyktu's ~35% at comparable timepoints. But BMS will counter with safety data and physician comfort.

Amgen — Otezla ($2.3B FY2025, -5%)

Amgen FY2025 call (January 2026):

  1. Managed decline confirmed: Otezla declined 5% to $2.3B. Amgen's promotional spend on Otezla is minimal — the product is in harvest mode as biologics dominate the moderate psoriasis market.

  2. Amgen framing Otezla as "complementary to biologics": Rather than fighting the decline, Amgen is positioning Otezla as a first-line oral option before biologic escalation. This creates a natural funnel where Otezla patients eventually step up to TREMFYA, Skyrizi, or ICOTYDE.

  3. ICOTYDE is the clear successor: For patients who want oral convenience plus biologic-class efficacy, ICOTYDE makes Otezla obsolete. The question is whether payers agree and create formulary pathways.

Implication for J&J: Otezla's declining base is ICOTYDE's primary capture pool in the first 12 months. J&J should position ICOTYDE as "everything Otezla was supposed to be" — oral, convenient, but with IL-23 efficacy.

Eli Lilly — Taltz ($3.0B FY2025, flat)

Lilly FY2025 call (January 2026):

  1. Taltz is a holding pattern: Flat growth at ~$3B reflects a mature product with minimal promotional investment. Lilly's earnings call barely mentioned Taltz — GLP-1 dominance consumes all strategic oxygen.

  2. Taltz switches are an opportunity pool: As a single IL-17A agent, Taltz lacks the dual mechanism of Bimzelx and the multi-domain breadth of IL-23. Dermatologists may look to trade up — the question is whether they go to Bimzelx (within IL-17) or TREMFYA/Skyrizi (to IL-23).

Implication for J&J: Taltz attrition patients are a contested switching pool. ZoomRx PET should track where Taltz patients go when they switch — IL-23 vs. dual IL-17 vs. oral.

Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Us

  1. The immunology market grew to ~$42B in FY2025 and is accelerating — AbbVie alone generated $32.8B from Skyrizi+Rinvoq+Humira. J&J's $5B TREMFYA is still <15% of AbbVie's immunology revenue. The gap is structural.

  2. GI is the highest-growth segment and TREMFYA's best competitive vector — Skyrizi leads within IL-23, but IL-23 as a class is displacing TNF and JAK in IBD. TREMFYA's "new-start share" claim in Q1 2026 confirms this strategy is working.

  3. ICOTYDE enters a contested oral market — Sotyktu ($400M, +90%), Otezla ($2.3B, declining), and eventually ICOTYDE will define the oral psoriasis category. BMS is already positioning against ICOTYDE. First full-year performance will determine the oral IL-23 thesis.

  4. The IL-17 class is bifurcating — Bimzelx growing, Cosentyx slowing, Taltz flat. Dual IL-17 is winning; single IL-17 is losing. This favors IL-23 as the next-generation biologic class in multi-domain patients.

3. Clinical/Regulatory

  • TREMFYA real-world evidence in GI accumulating; long-term safety data strengthening the IL-23 narrative
  • ICOTYDE Phase 3 published: 70% IGA 0/1 at Week 16 with placebo-level safety — strong launch profile
  • ICOTYDE FDA filing complete; PDUFA expected Q1 2026
  • TL1A inhibitor class emerging in GI pipeline (Roche, Merck, others) — watch for Phase 3 readouts in 2026
  • Skyrizi SEQUENCE data (head-to-head vs. Stelara in CD) continues as AbbVie's primary promotional weapon in GI

4. Thesis Update

Thesis Status Evidence This Quarter
1. TREMFYA GI growth can overcome Stelara erosion ON TRACK 65% growth, $5B annual — but segment still declining; needs 2+ more quarters of 50%+ growth
2. AbbVie's scale advantage is structural CONFIRMED $17.3B Skyrizi vs. $5B TREMFYA; AbbVie's Humira transition complete, J&J's Stelara transition still in progress
3. ICOTYDE can define the oral biologic category PRE-LAUNCH Filing complete; Sotyktu at $400M proving the category exists; Otezla declining creates switching pool
4. IL-17 class is losing ground to IL-23 PARTIALLY CONFIRMED Cosentyx slowing, Taltz flat — but Bimzelx growing 70%, so dual IL-17 is the exception
5. Multi-domain (skin+GI) is TREMFYA's moat STRENGTHENED No IL-17 agent has GI approval; Rinvoq is the only non-IL-23 competitor in GI; TREMFYA's multi-domain span is unmatched

5. What to Watch Next Quarter (Q1 2026)

  1. ICOTYDE PDUFA decision — approval expected Q1 2026; the launch execution in the first weeks will set the trajectory
  2. AbbVie Q1 2026 Skyrizi — sustaining 50%+ growth at $17B+ base would be extraordinary; any deceleration is a signal
  3. TREMFYA Q1 2026 revenue — needs >$1.2B quarterly to stay on track for $6B+ FY2026
  4. Sotyktu Q1 2026 — does it continue accelerating into ICOTYDE's launch? First competitive overlap quarter
  5. Stelara erosion rate — how fast is the base disappearing? Determines the immunology segment's trajectory

6. Conversation Starters for J&J

  1. "AbbVie's Skyrizi ended at $17.3B — 3.5x TREMFYA. But your growth rate is higher and GI is where the market is moving. The question is whether TREMFYA can build an installed base in GI before AbbVie's promotional intensity locks in Skyrizi as the default. That's a 12-month window, and we should be measuring algorithm stickiness now." — Frames the urgency.

  2. "Sotyktu hit $400M with 90% growth — and they're already positioning against ICOTYDE. The oral biologic category is real. J&J needs to own it with ICOTYDE's efficacy story, not just convenience. We can track ICOTYDE vs. Sotyktu head-to-head perception from launch week." — Surfaces the competitive threat.

  3. "Bimzelx grew to €1.3B by being the best-in-class injectable in derm. TREMFYA doesn't beat Bimzelx in skin clearance — but it beats everything in multi-domain patients. Your promotional messaging needs to segment: ICOTYDE for convenience seekers, TREMFYA for multi-domain complexity. We can measure whether the field force is executing this segmentation." — Data-driven portfolio strategy.

Sources: J&J Q4 2025 / FY2025 earnings call (Jan 2026), AbbVie Q4 2025 / FY2025 (Jan 2026), Novartis FY2025 (Jan 2026), BMS FY2025 (Jan 2026), UCB FY2025 (Feb 2026), Lilly FY2025 (Jan 2026), Amgen FY2025 (Jan 2026).

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home

Amgen — Otezla ($2.3B FY2025, -5%)
Amgen FY2025 call (January 2026):

Amgen framing Otezla as "complementary to biologics": Rather than fighting the decline, Amgen is positioning Otezla as a first-line oral option before biologic escalation. This creates a natural funnel where Otezla patients eventually step up to TREMFYA, Skyrizi, or ICOTYDE.

GI is the highest-growth segment and TREMFYA's best competitive vector — Skyrizi leads within IL-23, but IL-23 as a class is displacing TNF and JAK in IBD. TREMFYA's "new-start share" claim in Q1 2026 confirms this strategy is working.


ICOTYDE enters a contested oral market — Sotyktu ($400M, +90%), Otezla ($2.3B, declining), and eventually ICOTYDE will define the oral psoriasis category. BMS is already positioning against ICOTYDE. First full-year performance will determine the oral IL-23 thesis.


The IL-17 class is bifurcating — Bimzelx growing, Cosentyx slowing, Taltz flat. Dual IL-17 is winning; single IL-17 is losing. This favors IL-23 as the next-generation biologic class in multi-domain patients.



3. Clinical/Regulatory

TREMFYA real-world evidence in GI accumulating; long-term safety data strengthening the IL-23 narrative
ICOTYDE Phase 3 published: 70% IGA 0/1 at Week 16 with placebo-level safety — strong launch profile
ICOTYDE FDA filing complete; PDUFA expected Q1 2026
TL1A inhibitor class emerging in GI pipeline (Roche, Merck, others) — watch for Phase 3 readouts in 2026
Skyrizi SEQUENCE data (head-to-head vs. Stelara in CD) continues as AbbVie's primary promotional weapon in GI
